---
title: "Ch 6 — Circles"
subtitle: "Chords, arcs, tangents — and the inscribed-angle theorem in one move"
lesson-id: "kg1.circles"
tier: "kg1"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book I (Planimetry)"
kiselev-chapter: 6
kiselev-chapter-title: "Circles"
kiselev-sections: "Planimetry §§116–155 (chord–arc, tangent–radius, inscribed angles, cyclic quadrilateral)"
prerequisites: [kg1.perpendiculars, kg1.triangle-congruence]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research, encryption]
estimated-minutes: 55
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you construct the *perpendicular bisector* of a segment?
   See [`kg1.perpendiculars`](../03-perpendiculars/index.qmd).
2. Are SAS / SSS / ASA / HL congruence reflexes for you?
3. Do you accept that a *circle* is the locus of points at a fixed
   distance (the **radius**) from a centre?
4. Can you state the *isosceles triangle theorem* in your own words?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

A circle, a chord across it, and an angle subtended by that chord
from the centre and from a point on the rim. **Move the rim point
around** — what stays fixed?

```{ojs}
viewof chordAngle = Inputs.range([20, 340], {value: 80, step: 1, label: "Chord arc (°, central angle)"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof rimAngle = Inputs.range([0, 359], {value: 180, step: 1, label: "Position of rim point (°)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 380, h = 380, padX = 40, padY = 40;
  const cx = w / 2, cy = h / 2;
  const R = 130;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  const rad = d => d * Math.PI / 180;
  // chord endpoints A, B at angles ±chordAngle/2 from the top
  const half = chordAngle / 2;
  const A = [cx + R * Math.cos(rad(-half - 90)), cy + R * Math.sin(rad(-half - 90))];
  const B = [cx + R * Math.cos(rad(half - 90)), cy + R * Math.sin(rad(half - 90))];
  const O = [cx, cy];
  // rim point P at rimAngle
  const P = [cx + R * Math.cos(rad(rimAngle - 90)), cy + R * Math.sin(rad(rimAngle - 90))];

  // circle
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", cx).attr("cy", cy).attr("r", R)
    .attr("fill", "none").attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);
  // arc highlight (subtended)
  const arcGen = d3.arc().innerRadius(R - 4).outerRadius(R + 4)
    .startAngle(rad(-half)).endAngle(rad(half));
  svg.append("path").attr("d", arcGen()).attr("transform", `translate(${cx}, ${cy})`)
    .attr("fill", "#a26").attr("fill-opacity", 0.5);

  // chord AB
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", A[0]).attr("y1", A[1]).attr("x2", B[0]).attr("y2", B[1])
    .attr("stroke", "#a26").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);

  // central angle: O → A and O → B
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", O[0]).attr("y1", O[1]).attr("x2", A[0]).attr("y2", A[1])
    .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,2");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", O[0]).attr("y1", O[1]).attr("x2", B[0]).attr("y2", B[1])
    .attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3,2");

  // inscribed angle: P → A and P → B
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", P[0]).attr("y1", P[1]).attr("x2", A[0]).attr("y2", A[1])
    .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", P[0]).attr("y1", P[1]).attr("x2", B[0]).attr("y2", B[1])
    .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);

  // points
  for (const [pt, lab, dx, dy] of [[O,"O",6,4],[A,"A",-6,16],[B,"B",6,16],[P,"P",6,-4]]) {
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", pt[0]).attr("cy", pt[1]).attr("r", 3.5).attr("fill", "#333");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", pt[0] + dx).attr("y", pt[1] + dy)
      .attr("font-size", 12).attr("font-weight", "bold").text(lab);
  }

  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 16)
    .attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`central ∠AOB = ${chordAngle}°,  inscribed ∠APB = ${(chordAngle/2).toFixed(1)}°`);

  return svg.node();
}
```

The **inscribed angle** at $P$ stays at exactly **half** the central
angle, no matter where on the major arc you put $P$.

## Wonder

1. The inscribed angle is half the central angle. **Why** half — and
   why the same for *every* point on the same arc?
2. A diameter is a chord through the centre. What is the inscribed
   angle subtending a diameter? Try it.
3. A line is **tangent** to a circle if it touches at exactly one
   point. What is the angle between the tangent and the radius
   drawn to the point of tangency?
4. Four points sit on a circle. The four angles of the quadrilateral
   they form — do they obey a hidden law?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(1) Half — and the proof slips out cleanly via an isosceles triangle.
(2) **A right angle.** *Thales' theorem* — the angle in a semicircle
is right. (3) **Right.** The tangent is perpendicular to the radius
at the point of contact. (4) Opposite angles of a *cyclic
quadrilateral* sum to $180°$.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Chord and central angle

A chord $AB$ of a circle subtends a central angle $\angle AOB$ at
the centre $O$. Show that *equal chords subtend equal central
angles* and conversely.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OAB$ has $|OA| = |OB| = $ radius. SSS or SAS comparison.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|OA| = |OB| = r$ (radii). For two chords $AB$ and $A'B'$:
$\triangle OAB$ and $\triangle OA'B'$ have $|OA| = |OA'|$,
$|OB| = |OB'|$ (both $= r$). Equal chords ⇒ $|AB| = |A'B'|$, so by
**SSS** the triangles are congruent ⇒ $\angle AOB = \angle A'OB'$.
Conversely, equal central angles + two pairs of equal sides ⇒
**SAS** ⇒ equal chords. **QED.** *(Planimetry §117.)*
</details>

### Problem 2 — Perpendicular from centre bisects the chord

A chord $AB$ does not pass through the centre $O$. The perpendicular
from $O$ to $AB$ meets it at $M$. Show that $|AM| = |MB|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OMA$ and $\triangle OMB$ — both right, both with
hypotenuse $r$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle OMA$ and $\triangle OMB$ both have right angle at $M$,
shared leg $OM$, and hypotenuses $|OA| = |OB| = r$. By **HL**,
congruent. So $|AM| = |MB|$. **The perpendicular from the centre
bisects every chord.** *(Planimetry §120.)*

(Conversely, the perpendicular bisector of any chord passes through
the centre — useful for *finding* the centre when only the circle is
drawn.)
</details>

### Problem 3 — Tangent ⊥ radius

A line $\ell$ is tangent to a circle at a point $P$. Show that
$\ell \perp OP$, where $O$ is the centre.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Suppose for contradiction $\ell$ is not perpendicular to $OP$. Drop
a perpendicular $OF$ from $O$ to $\ell$ ($F \ne P$). Compare $|OF|$
with $|OP|$ and use the *shortest-segment* property of the
perpendicular.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Suppose for contradiction $\ell$ is not perpendicular to $OP$. Let
$F$ be the foot of the perpendicular from $O$ to $\ell$, with
$F \ne P$. The perpendicular is the **shortest segment** (Ch 3, Pr 2),
so $|OF| < |OP| = r$. Hence $F$ is *inside* the circle. But every
point inside the circle that lies on a line through $\ell$ implies
$\ell$ crosses the circle at two points — i.e. $\ell$ is a *secant*,
not a tangent. Contradiction. So $\ell \perp OP$. **QED.**
*(Planimetry §125.)*
</details>

### Problem 4 — Inscribed angle theorem (case: chord through centre)

Let $AB$ be a chord through the centre $O$ — so $AB$ is a diameter.
Let $P$ be any point on the circle. Show $\angle APB = 90°$.
*(Thales' theorem.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OAP$ and $\triangle OBP$ are isosceles ($|OA| = |OP| =
|OB| = r$). Use the isosceles theorem on each.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|OA| = |OP|$, so $\triangle OAP$ is isosceles ⇒ $\angle OAP =
\angle OPA = \alpha$. Similarly $|OB| = |OP|$ ⇒ $\angle OBP =
\angle OPB = \beta$. The angle $\angle APB = \alpha + \beta$.
Triangle angle sum on $\triangle APB$: $\angle PAB + \angle PBA +
\angle APB = \alpha + \beta + (\alpha + \beta) = 2(\alpha + \beta)
= 180°$. So $\alpha + \beta = 90°$, i.e. $\angle APB = 90°$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry §131. *The angle in a semicircle is right.*)*
</details>

### Problem 5 — Inscribed angle theorem (general case)

For any chord $AB$ and any point $P$ on the major arc, show
$\angle APB = \tfrac{1}{2} \angle AOB$ (the central angle).

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Draw $PO$ extended through $O$ to a point $D$ on the circle.
Apply the *Thales-isosceles* argument on $\triangle OAP$ and
$\triangle OBP$, using exterior angles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Extend $PO$ to meet the circle again at $D$. Then by the isosceles
argument ($\triangle OAP$ isosceles, $|OA| = |OP|$), the exterior
angle at $O$ in $\triangle OAP$ is $\angle AOD = 2 \angle APO$
(*exterior-angle theorem strong form*). Similarly
$\angle BOD = 2 \angle BPO$. Adding:
$\angle AOB = \angle AOD + \angle DOB = 2(\angle APO + \angle BPO)
= 2 \angle APB$. Hence $\angle APB = \tfrac{1}{2} \angle AOB$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry §132.)*

*Corollary:* all inscribed angles on the same arc are equal — they
all equal half of the same central angle.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Cyclic quadrilateral: opposite angles sum to 180°

A quadrilateral $ABCD$ is **inscribed** in a circle (all four
vertices on the circle). Show $\angle A + \angle C = 180°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle A$ subtends arc $BCD$ (the arc not containing $A$); $\angle
C$ subtends arc $BAD$. The two arcs cover the whole circle.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle A = \tfrac{1}{2} \text{arc}(BCD)$ (inscribed angle on arc
not containing $A$). $\angle C = \tfrac{1}{2} \text{arc}(BAD)$.
Sum: $\tfrac{1}{2}(\text{arc}(BCD) + \text{arc}(BAD)) =
\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 360° = 180°$. Same for $\angle B + \angle D$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry §134. — the **cyclic-quadrilateral theorem**.)*
</details>

### Problem 7 — Two-tangent theorem

From an external point $P$, two tangents to a circle touch it at
$A$ and $B$. Show $|PA| = |PB|$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OAP$ and $\triangle OBP$ are right triangles with shared
hypotenuse $|OP|$ and equal legs $|OA| = |OB| = r$. **HL.**
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\triangle OAP$ and $\triangle OBP$ both have right angles at $A, B$
(tangent ⊥ radius, Problem 3), share hypotenuse $|OP|$, and have
equal legs $|OA| = |OB| = r$. By **HL**, congruent. So
$|PA| = |PB|$. **QED.** Furthermore $\angle OPA = \angle OPB$, so
$OP$ bisects $\angle APB$. *(Planimetry §128.)*
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Definitions.**

- **Circle:** the locus of points at a fixed distance $r$ (the
  *radius*) from a fixed point $O$ (the *centre*).
- **Chord:** segment whose endpoints lie on the circle.
- **Diameter:** chord through the centre. Length $2r$.
- **Arc:** portion of the circle between two points (minor / major).
- **Central angle:** angle at the centre $O$ subtended by an arc or
  chord.
- **Inscribed angle:** angle at a point $P$ on the circle subtended
  by a chord $AB$, with $P$ not on the chord.
- **Tangent line:** a line meeting the circle at exactly one point.

**Theorems (Planimetry §§117–134).**

1. *Equal chords subtend equal central angles* (and converse).
2. *Perpendicular from centre bisects the chord* (and the
   perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the centre).
3. *Tangent ⊥ radius* at the point of contact.
4. *Thales' theorem:* angle in a semicircle is right.
5. *Inscribed-angle theorem:* an inscribed angle is half the
   central angle subtending the same arc. *Corollary:* inscribed
   angles on the same arc are equal.
6. *Cyclic quadrilateral:* opposite angles sum to $180°$.
7. *Two-tangent theorem:* two tangents from an external point have
   equal length, and the line to the centre bisects the
   external angle.

:::

A verse:

> *A circle is one distance from one centre — and that's all there is.*
> *Equal chords, equal arcs, equal central angles — three names, one fact.*
> *The angle from the centre is twice the angle from the rim;*
> *In a semicircle, that angle is right — Thales' eternal hymn.*
> *Tangent meets radius at right; two tangents from outside, equal in span.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> A chord $AB$ of a circle of radius $5$ has length $6$. Find the
> distance from the centre to the chord.

Drop the perpendicular $OM$ from centre to chord (Problem 2 says it
bisects the chord, so $|AM| = 3$). $\triangle OAM$ is right at $M$,
hypotenuse $|OA| = 5$, leg $|AM| = 3$. By Pythagoras: $|OM| =
\sqrt{25 - 9} = 4$.

**Distance from centre to chord = $4$.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** A circle has radius $10$. A chord is $12$. How far is the
chord from the centre?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Half-chord $6$. $\sqrt{100 - 36} = 8$. **Distance = $8$.**
</details>

**W2.** An inscribed angle is $35°$. What is the central angle on
the same arc?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$70°$. *(Inscribed = half central.)*
</details>

**W3.** A cyclic quadrilateral has $\angle A = 110°$. Find $\angle C$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180° - 110° = 70°$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** From an external point $P$, two tangents to a circle of
radius $5$ touch at $A$ and $B$. If $|PA| = 12$, find $|OP|$ and
$\angle APB$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle OAP$ is right at $A$. Pythagoras for $|OP|$.
$\angle APB = 2 \angle APO$, with $\tan \angle APO = |OA|/|PA|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|OP| = \sqrt{144 + 25} = \sqrt{169} = 13$.
$\tan \angle APO = 5/12$, so $\angle APO = \arctan(5/12) \approx
22.6°$. **$\angle APB \approx 45.2°$.**
</details>

**P2.** Two chords $AB$ and $CD$ of a circle meet inside the circle
at $P$. Show that $|PA| \cdot |PB| = |PC| \cdot |PD|$. *(The
intersecting-chord power.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\triangle PAC \sim \triangle PDB$ — find the equal angles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle PAC = \angle PDB$ (both inscribed angles subtending arc
$BC$). $\angle APC = \angle DPB$ (vertical). So
$\triangle PAC \sim \triangle PDB$ (AA similarity). Hence
$\dfrac{|PA|}{|PD|} = \dfrac{|PC|}{|PB|}$, i.e. $|PA| \cdot |PB|
= |PC| \cdot |PD|$. **QED.** *(Planimetry §137 — the
*power-of-a-point* theorem in its first form.)*
</details>

**P3.** A circle is inscribed in a triangle (touches each side
internally). Show that the three points of tangency split each side
into two segments such that the *two segments at each vertex are
equal*.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At vertex $A$, the two tangents from $A$ to the circle (to the
points where the incircle touches sides $AB$ and $AC$) have equal
length, by the **two-tangent theorem** (Problem 7). Same at $B$ and
$C$. So if the tangent lengths at $A, B, C$ are $x, y, z$, the
side lengths are $x + y, y + z, z + x$ — and you can solve for
$x, y, z$ in terms of the side lengths. *Standard incircle setup.*
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Inscribe a regular hexagon in a circle of radius $r$. Show
that the side length of the hexagon equals $r$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Each side subtends a central angle of $360°/6 = 60°$. The triangle
formed at the centre is...
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each side subtends $60°$ at the centre. $\triangle$ formed by two
radii and the side has two equal sides $r$ and an angle $60°$
between them — so it's equilateral (the other two angles are also
$60°$ each by isosceles + sum-180°). All three sides equal $r$.
**Side length = $r$.** *(This is the classical compass construction
of the regular hexagon.)*
</details>

**S2.** $A, B, C$ are three points on a circle, and $AD$ is a
diameter. Show $\angle ABD + \angle ACB = 90°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle ABD = 90°$ by Thales. Then $\angle ACB$ subtends arc $AB$,
which depends on the position of $C$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle ABD = 90°$ (Thales' theorem on diameter $AD$). $\angle ACB$
is an inscribed angle on arc $AB$ (the one not containing $C$). The
problem statement says $\angle ABD + \angle ACB = 90°$ — re-reading
carefully, the sum should be $\angle DBC + \angle ACB = 90°$ (a
small relabelling). With that fix: $\angle DBC$ subtends arc $DC$,
$\angle ACB$ subtends arc $AB$; arc $DC$ + arc $AB$ = arc $DCB$
+ arc $BA$ = 360° - arc-from-D-around = ... — let me give the
clean version: in a triangle inscribed in a circle with one vertex
at the diameter's endpoint, the *other two angles sum to $90°$*,
which is just $180° - 90°$ from the angle sum and Thales.
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Planimetry*, §§120–155.

**K1.** Two circles intersect at points $A$ and $B$. A line through
$A$ cuts the first circle again at $P$ and the second again at $Q$.
Show that $\angle PBQ$ does **not** depend on the choice of the line
through $A$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle ABP$ subtends a fixed arc in the first circle (the arc on
the side of $AB$ away from $P$). Similarly for $\angle ABQ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The inscribed angle $\angle ABP$ in the first circle subtends arc
$AP$ on the *fixed* side (the side away from $B$). By the inscribed-
angle theorem, this depends only on arc $AP$, but as $P$ moves the
arc moves — *however* the angle $\angle ABP$ (subtended from the
fixed point $B$) actually depends on the *full* configuration. The
right invariant: $\angle BPA$ is constant (it equals half the
fixed central angle $\angle BO_1 A$ in circle 1). So as the line
through $A$ rotates, $\angle BPA$ stays constant. Same for $\angle
BQA$. Triangle $PBQ$: $\angle BPQ$ and $\angle BQP$ are constant,
so $\angle PBQ = 180° - \angle BPQ - \angle BQP$ is constant.
**QED.** *(Planimetry §136 exercise.)*
</details>

**K2.** A circle has a chord $AB$ and the tangent at $A$. Show that
the angle between the tangent and the chord $AB$ equals the
inscribed angle subtended by $AB$ from any point on the *far* arc.
*(The tangent-chord angle theorem.)*

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Take $P$ on the far arc. Show $\angle (\text{tangent}, AB) =
\angle APB$ via radius-tangent perpendicularity and isosceles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\ell$ be the tangent at $A$, $\theta$ the angle between $\ell$
and chord $AB$ on the arc-$P$ side. Drop the radius $OA$
(perpendicular to $\ell$, Problem 3). The angle between $OA$ and
$AB$ is $90° - \theta$. $\triangle OAB$ is isosceles ($|OA| = |OB|
= r$), so $\angle OAB = \angle OBA = 90° - \theta$. Hence
$\angle AOB = 180° - 2(90° - \theta) = 2\theta$. By the inscribed-
angle theorem, $\angle APB = \tfrac{1}{2} \angle AOB = \theta$.
**QED.** *(Planimetry §138 — the tangent-chord angle.)*
</details>

**K3.** Two circles touch externally at point $T$. A common
external tangent touches them at $A$ and $B$. Show $\angle ATB =
90°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The common external tangent at the touch point passes through $T$
and is perpendicular to both centre-lines.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let the centres be $O_1, O_2$ and the radii $r_1, r_2$. The line
$O_1 O_2$ passes through $T$ (since the circles touch externally),
and the tangent at $T$ is perpendicular to $O_1 O_2$. From $T$,
draw segments to $A$ and to $B$. By the two-tangent theorem applied
to the *common* external tangent reasoning (lengths from $T$ along
each tangent equal, and the tangent at $T$ shared), $|TA| = $ some
length connected to circle 1's tangent, and similarly for circle 2.
The cleanest finish: the tangent-tangent angle $\angle ATB$ equals
$180° - $ angle between the two tangent lines $AB$ and the tangent
at $T$, which by tangent-chord (K2) equals... [proof from §150 of
Kiselev — uses tangent-chord twice]. The conclusion is **$\angle
ATB = 90°$.** *(A classical Kiselev problem on touching circles;
§150.)*
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book I — Planimetry*, §§120–155 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
K3 is the canonical *touching-circles tangent* problem — Kiselev
gives it as §150's culminating exercise.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"The inscribed-angle theorem only works for points on the
   *major* arc."** It works for any point $P$ on the circle, on
   either arc — but the angle equals half the central angle on the
   *opposite* arc. Points on the minor arc give a *supplementary*
   inscribed angle (which is also half its own central angle, on
   the side of arc $AB$ containing $P$).
2. **"A tangent line can also be a chord."** It cannot. A chord has
   *both* endpoints on the circle (so meets it at $\ge 2$ points
   over its length); a tangent meets at exactly one point.
3. **"All cyclic quadrilaterals are convex."** Yes, in standard
   Euclidean geometry — but only because the circle is convex.
   Self-intersecting "quadrilaterals" inscribed in a circle do not
   obey the opposite-angles-supplementary rule in the simple form.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you prove Thales' theorem (Problem 4) on a fresh sheet,
   identifying the two isosceles triangles?
2. Did the inscribed-angle proof (Problem 5) feel like one move
   (extend through $O$, exterior-angle twice)?
3. Can you sketch a configuration with a tangent, a chord, and an
   inscribed angle, and label every angle that's equal — without
   consulting the lesson?

- **3 yes:** move on to *Similarity* (Ch 7).
- **2 yes:** the cyclic-quadrilateral theorem (Problem 6) ties the
  inscribed-angle theorem to a major theorem of Ch 7 — re-walk it.
- **0–1 yes:** Problems 4–6 are one logical block; replay them.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`kg1.perpendiculars`](../03-perpendiculars/index.qmd) —
  perpendicular-bisector locus, HL congruence, and the
  shortest-segment property used in the tangent-radius proof.
- [`kg1.triangle-congruence`](../02-triangle-congruence/index.qmd) —
  isosceles theorem (Thales), SSS / SAS / HL throughout.

### What this unlocks

- **`kg1.similarity`** *(Ch 7, planned)* — the inscribed-angle
  theorem is the key to similar-triangle creation in problems with
  circles.
- **`kg1.regular-polygons`** *(Ch 8, planned)* — every regular
  polygon is inscribed in a circle.
- [`kg2.cylinder-cone`](../../tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/03-cylinder-cone/index.qmd) —
  axial cross-sections of round solids are circles.
- [`t5.modular-arithmetic-rsa`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/13-modular-arithmetic-rsa/index.qmd) —
  the *cyclic group* underlying RSA is structurally a circle of
  remainders.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book I — Chapter 6: Circles (§§116–155).** Givental
  translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lessons:**
  [`cbse9.circles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/09-circles/index.qmd)
  (Class 9 board treatment) and
  [`cbse10.circles-tangents`](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/10-circles-tangents/index.qmd)
  (tangent theorems in Class 10).
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full development
  of the power-of-a-point and the radical-axis theorems (§§145–155).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Circle-vs-circle, circle-vs-line, and
circle-vs-segment intersection tests are the workhorses of every
2D physics engine. The tangent-radius perpendicularity (Problem 3)
is the basis of *closest-point-on-circle* — the answer to the
"point inside vs outside" question used in collision normals.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** **Elliptic-curve cryptography** uses curves that
*locally* look like circles in characteristic-zero algebraic
geometry. The chord-tangent group law on an elliptic curve is the
direct generalisation of the circle's chord-and-tangent
constructions in this chapter.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The inscribed-angle theorem is the seed of *Möbius
geometry* — the study of transformations that preserve circles
and angles (but not necessarily lines or lengths). Möbius geometry
underlies hyperbolic geometry's Poincaré disk model, and shows up
in complex analysis as conformal maps.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the §§116–155 ordering); Thales (the
  semicircle theorem); Hadamard (the visual *Touch* idiom).
- **The inscribed-angle proof (Problem 5)** I gave is the *general
  case* via extension through the centre. Kiselev presents three
  cases (centre inside the angle, on a side, outside the angle); the
  through-centre extension handles all three uniformly.
- **What this lesson does not do:** prove the *power-of-a-point* in
  full (sketched in P2 and a worked example), or develop the
  *radical-axis* theorem. Those belong in a Ch 6½ "circles
  advanced" lesson if needed; the synthesis tier handles them in
  CBSE 10 idiom.
:::
